model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With a fixed budget of 256 steps (well under one epoch), the baseline Adam learning rate is likely conservative. Increasing LR is a high-leverage, low-complexity change that often improves early optimization speed and validation loss in short-horizon CIFAR-10 training.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The previous step showed the schedule harmed this short-horizon run. With only 256 steps, reducing regularization pressure by removing weight decay can improve early fit and validation loss, especially with Adam on a small CNN.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The model is very small for CIFAR-10; increasing base channels is a minimal capacity boost that can improve representation quality within the same 256-step budget while keeping architecture simple.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After a capacity increase in convolutional features improved loss, the next likely bottleneck is the small MLP head. Doubling the hidden width is a simple, targeted capacity increase that can improve class separation with minimal training-loop complexity change.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After increasing model capacity successfully, a modest learning-rate increase is a low-complexity way to improve optimization progress within the fixed 256-step budget without changing training logic.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The recent LR increase degraded validation loss, indicating this regime is already near the useful step-size ceiling. Reverting to 1e-3 restores the better-performing optimization scale before trying other axes.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"With the current larger model, switching to AdamW introduces decoupled weight decay, which often improves generalization stability versus Adam at similar step budgets. This is a compact optimizer-family test with minimal code complexity.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"AdamW helped at 1e-4 weight decay; a slightly stronger decay is a simple follow-up to test whether additional regularization further improves validation loss for the expanded-capacity model.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"A larger batch can improve signal quality per optimization step under a fixed 256-step budget, effectively using more unique examples. Reverting to the better prior decay avoids carrying the degradation from the previous regularization change.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The large gain from moving to batch size 128 suggests the run is data-exposure-limited under fixed steps. Increasing batch size further is a direct way to expose more examples within the same 256 updates while keeping the rest of the recipe stable.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Performance has improved consistently with larger batch sizes, indicating the fixed-step budget benefits from more examples per update. Pushing to 256 is the next minimal extension of that trend.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Validation loss has improved consistently as batch size increased, suggesting this fixed-step setting benefits from more examples per update. Moving from 256 to 320 is a moderate next increase to continue that trend while limiting step-time growth.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size 320 slightly regressed from the best result, so reverting to 256 restores the stronger operating point before exploring other axes.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"At the current stronger setting (AdamW + large batch), a short-horizon cosine decay may help late-step refinement over 256 updates while keeping all other factors fixed.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The cosine schedule caused a large regression in this configuration, so disabling it restores the previously better constant-LR regime before further tuning.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"With batch size now much larger (256), the prior LR may be conservative. A modest LR increase is a focused test to improve convergence speed within the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase improved validation loss at the current strong configuration, so a further modest increase is a targeted continuation to find the best short-horizon step size.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.6e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Validation loss continued improving as LR increased from 1e-3 to 1.2e-3 to 1.4e-3, so the next efficient move is another small increase to probe the short-budget optimum.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.8e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.6e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Loss has improved monotonically across recent learning-rate increases at the same configuration, so the final step should continue this trajectory with one more moderate LR increase to target the short-budget optimum.","secondary_modes":[]}
